questions.question: What is the 'Value Trade' principle in negotiation?

options.text: Trading your product for another company's product.
Never giving a price concession without receiving a concession (e.g., terms, commitment, or marketing rights) in return.
Giving the customer exactly what they want so they like you.
Trading features for a higher price.

questions.explanation: If you give a discount without a trade, you signal that your original price was arbitrary. A trade maintains the integrity of your pricing while providing the prospect with a 'win'.

questions.question: Which of these is considered a 'Low-Cost, High-Value' get for a solo founder?

options.text: A public case study and agreement to be a reference.
A handwritten thank-you note from the CEO.
A 5% higher price but with double the support hours.
A free company t-shirt.

questions.explanation: Social proof (case studies) is incredibly valuable for future lead generation and closing, yet it costs the prospect very little to provide once they are a happy customer.

questions.question: What is the risk of simply saying 'Sure, I can do 10% off' without a trade?

options.text: The prospect will feel bad for you.
It inadvertently signals that your original price was inflated and invites further negotiation.
It makes the proposal too short.
There is no risk; it's a standard business practice.

questions.explanation: Immediate discounting tells the buyer that your price is a 'negotiable suggestion' rather than a reflection of value. It teaches them to keep pushing for more.

questions.question: If a prospect wants a lower price and you decide to 'Reduce Scope,' what are you doing?

options.text: Removing certain features or services to match their lower budget.
Giving them the full product but charging them less.
Adding more features for the same price.
Ignoring their request and sending the same proposal again.

questions.explanation: Scope reduction is the most logical form of pricing flexibility. 'If you can't afford the full solution, we can start with a smaller part of it.'

questions.question: A discount request is often considered a 'Buying Signal' because:

options.text: People who don't want to buy don't bother negotiating the terms.
It proves the customer is running out of money.
It shows the customer is trying to distract you.
It is a requirement in some industries.

questions.explanation: Negotiation requires effort. A prospect only invests that effort if they see a future where they use your product. They are essentially asking for a way to say 'yes' that fits their internal constraints.

questions.question: Identify one 'Low-Cost, High-Value' item (e.g., a testimonial, an annual payment, or a referral) that would be specifically valuable for your business right now. How would you phrase the 'Value Trade' for it?
questions.aiPrompt: Evaluate if the user identifies a specific trade item and uses 'If/Then' language (e.g., 'If I do X, then will you do Y?'). Provide feedback on how this trade protects their perceived value.
